file.content:
# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

A markdown list of Tasks currently in progress


## Todo

A markdown list of task to do in the near feature

 - Why is `jj log --ignore-immutable --limit 100` not showing the all commits on main, `gitk` does?
 - Use the git trailers, https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers, format for adding inter and intra repo information in commits
 - Determine the organization of the commits in jj and how we can iterate over them.
   - I'd like to beable to see "all" commits organized appropriately, `gitk --all` sees many more commits than any jj command I've found

## Done

See older [done.md](done.md).

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed.

- Create a binary that lists jj info[[1]]


# References

A set of markdown references for tasks and details including vc changeID URLs.
See [ChangeID footer syntax](chores-01.md#changeid-footer-syntax).

[1]: /notes/chores-01.md#create-a-binary-that-lists-jj-info

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture. Here's what's going on:

**Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`:**

1. **`jj log` shows 9 logical commits** (the linear history from `@` back to `root()`). Even with `--ignore-immutable --limit 100`, it only shows the revisions in its default revset (visible, non-hidden commits).

2. **`gitk --all` / `git log --all` shows 123 commits** because jj stores all previous versions of rewritten commits under `refs/jj/keep/*`. Every time jj rewrites a commit (rebase, amend, squash, etc.), the old git commit is preserved as a keep ref. These are **hidden/obsolete predecessors** that jj intentionally hides from its log.

3. The git graph shows many duplicate commit messages on parallel branches — these are the old versions of the same jj change that were rewritten over time.

**Key insight:** jj's model tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits. When a change is rewritten, the old git commit becomes hidden but is kept in git for safety. `jj log` shows the current state of each change; `gitk` shows every git commit including all the obsolete ones.

**To see hidden/obsolete commits in jj:**

content.text:
This confirms it. Here's the summary:

## Answer

**`jj log` is working correctly** — it shows the 9 *current* logical commits in the repo. The `--ignore-immutable` flag only controls which commits are shown as mutable (editable), not which ones are visible.

**`gitk --all` shows 123 commits** because jj keeps old versions of rewritten commits as `refs/jj/keep/*` refs in git. Every `jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc. creates a new git commit and preserves the old one. For example, `nuzmqoko` ("Simplify README.md") alone has **9+ previous versions** visible via `jj obslog`.

**Useful jj commands to see more:**
- `jj obslog -r <change-id>` — shows the evolution history of a single change
- `jj log --revisions 'all()'` — shows all non-hidden commits (same as default for this repo)
- `jj op log` — shows operation history (each rewrite operation)

**The 123 `refs/jj/keep/*` refs are essentially jj's "undo history"** — they exist so `jj undo`/`jj op restore` can recover previous states. They aren't separate branches or meaningful commits; they're implementation artifacts.

Want me to update the todo with this finding?

content.text:
Yes, `jj log -r ::@` shows all ancestors of `@` (the working copy) — which in this linear repo gives the same result as `all()`. It shows all 9 real commits plus root, without the 123 obsolete keep refs that `gitk --all` displays.

Note: `::@` means "all ancestors of @". If you ever have multiple branches/heads, you'd need `jj log -r 'all()'` to see commits not ancestral to `@`. But for a single-branch workflow like this, `::@` covers everything.

file.content:
# vc-x1

This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
and one with the conversation with the bot.

I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
most importantly **why** the code evolved.

The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
own IDE for vc.

See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
doing so and I then created this README.md file.

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
doing so and I then created this README.md file.

## jj Tips for Git Users

If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
tools like `gitk --all`.

### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`

jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
shows the current version of each change.

### Useful commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
| `jj obslog -r <change-id>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |

In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.

## License

# vc-x1

This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
and one with the conversation with the bot.

I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
most importantly **why** the code evolved.

The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
own IDE for vc.

file.content:
# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Versioning during development

This is using jujustiu, jj + git and we'll see how it goes. Below is my
git workflow, jj will be different but we'll have to discover that as
we go.

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump directly to
  `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-devN`, implement
  across multiple commits, final commit removes `-devN`.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before starting.

For multi-step:
1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-devN` with a plan and commit as a chore marker
2. Implement in one or more `-devN` commits (bump N as needed)
3. Final commit removes `-devN`, updates todo/chores — this is the "done" marker

The final release commit (without `-devN`) signals completion rather than amending
prior commits. This keeps the git history readable and makes it easy to see which
commits were exploratory vs final.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions for hw-jjg-bot

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- App repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot session repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

## Committing

Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
visible at the start of the command.

### App repo
```
jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .
```

### Bot session repo
```
jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .claude
```

## jj Basics

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.

## Session End Workflows

When the user asks to "commit both repos" or says they're done, commit
both repos. Use the **same title** for both commits so they're easy to
correlate. The body can differ: the app repo body should summarize code
changes; the bot session repo body should note what was done in the
session.

```
jj commit -m "shared title" -m "app body" -R .
jj commit -m "shared title" -m "session body" -R .claude
```

When the user also asks to push, advance the `main` bookmarks to the
new commits first, then push.

```
jj bookmark set main -r @- -R .
jj bookmark set main -r @- -R .claude
jj git push -R .
jj git push -R .claude
```

content.text:

```markdown
## Pre-commit Requirements

### User approval

Never execute commit commands without the user's explicit approval.
Present the full commands for review first; only run them after the
user confirms.

### Versioning

Every change must start with a version bump. See
[Versioning during development](notes/README.md#versioning-during-development)
for details. Get user approval on single-step vs multi-step before starting.

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt -R .`
2. `cargo clippy -R .`
3. `cargo test -R .`
4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
5. Retest after install
```

Want me to add this to CLAUDE.md, or would you like to adjust the wording?

content.text:
OK, `0.2.0` is correct — the jj diff just displayed the lines oddly (the `1` is a line number prefix, not part of the version).

Here's the plan: squash `@` into `@-` (the previous commit `wtpmottv`) so everything is one commit, then describe it with the proper message. Here are the commands I'd run:

```bash
# Squash working copy into parent commit
jj squash -R .

# Describe the combined commit
jj describe -m "Add jj tips for git users to README" \
  -m "Add jj tips section to README explaining why jj log shows fewer commits than gitk. Add pre-commit requirements to CLAUDE.md. Fix clippy warning. Bump version to 0.2.0. Update todo." \
  -R .

# Commit bot session repo
jj commit -m "Add jj tips for git users to README" \
  -m "Session: investigated jj log vs gitk, documented findings in README, added pre-commit workflow to CLAUDE.md." \
  -R .claude
```

Approve?

file.content:
## Use footers to track changeIDs or notes

Commit footers can reference changeIDs and notes files using markdown
reference-link syntax. See [ChangeID footer syntax](#changeid-footer-syntax)
for the path rules.

Since jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time (not stored in the
repo), changeID references are only resolvable by tools that have access
to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).

## ChangeID footer syntax

Commit footers use markdown reference-link syntax to cross-reference
jj changeIDs across repos:

- `/changeID` — workspace-root repo (e.g. `/upxmvpyz`)
- `/<path>/changeID` — sub-repo within the workspace (e.g. `/.claude/vxmpmzsy`)

All changeID references must be workspace-root relative (start with `/`).

## Create a binary that lists jj info

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`

### Implementation

Created a Rust binary (`src/main.rs`) using `jj-lib` 0.39.0 that:

1. Opens the jj workspace from the current directory (or a path argument)

content.text:
I see — the existing convention uses markdown reference-link syntax for footers. But you said `ochid: <changeID>` — is `ochid` the trailer key you want (as in "other change ID"), or did you mean something else? And should each commit reference the *other* repo's changeID?

For example, the app repo commit would have:
```
ochid: /.claude/vupnzrpp
```
and the bot session commit would have:
```
ochid: /wtpmottv
```

Is that right?

message.content: yes ochid is "Other Change ID" and is our inter repo links, we're going to be moving to using "git trailer" style in our next commit but I want it added to this one.

content.text:

```bash
jj describe -r @- \
  -m "Add jj tips for git users to README" \
  -m "Add jj tips section to README explaining why jj log
shows fewer commits than gitk. Add pre-commit requirements
to CLAUDE.md. Fix clippy warning. Bump version to 0.2.0.
Update todo.

ochid: /.claude/vupnzrpp" \
  -R .

jj describe -r @- \
  -m "Add jj tips for git users to README" \
  -m "Session: investigated jj log vs gitk, documented
findings in README, added pre-commit workflow to CLAUDE.md.

ochid: /wtpmottv" \
  -R .claude
```

Approve?

message.content: Nice but one boo boo in .claude we have two commits "Add jj tips for git users to README", I'm guessing i'll need to make that change since you tweaking to yourself will result in a circular modifications?

content.text:
Right, you'll need to squash those two together in `.claude` since any change I make there would modify session data and create more changes. You can run:

```bash
jj squash -r @- -R .claude
```

That will fold the older "Add jj tips..." into its parent, leaving one commit with that title.

content.text:
Good to know — squashing in `.claude` is trickier since there are intermediate commits between the two with the same title. And the ochid fixup makes sense since the squash changes which commit the changeID points to.

Let me save this lesson for future reference.

file.content:
# Memory

## User Preferences

- **Commit approval**: Always present the full commit commands to the user for physical review BEFORE executing them. Do not run jj commit / git commit until the user explicitly approves.
- **Versioning**: Every plan must start with a version bump per notes/README.md "Versioning during development". Get user approval on single-step vs multi-step approach before starting.
- **Pre-commit checklist**: Before committing, run: tests, clippy, fmt, install the app, then retest.

## Key Files

- notes/README.md — contains versioning workflow, todo format rules
- notes/todo.md — near-term task tracking
- notes/chores-01.md — chore details and reference links
- CLAUDE.md — dual repo commit/push workflow

input.new_string:
## Lessons Learned

- **Avoid duplicate commits in .claude repo**: Committing the bot session repo multiple times in one session can create duplicate-titled commits with other session commits interleaved, making squashing non-trivial. Prefer a single commit to .claude at session end.
- **ochid trailers need updating after squash**: When squashing changes the target commit in .claude, the ochid reference in the app repo must be updated to point at the correct changeID.
- **Don't modify .claude repo programmatically mid-session**: Session data updates continuously, so bot-initiated changes to .claude can create circular modifications.

## Key Files

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

A markdown list of Tasks currently in progress


## Todo

A markdown list of task to do in the near feature

 - Use the git trailers, https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers, format for adding inter and intra repo information in commits
   - Add appropriate documentation for instance we must document throughly
     the <changeID-path> in `ochid: <changeID-path>.
     In particular `/` means workspace relative so `ochid: /wtpmottv` is not
     referencing .claude AI session repo but is refering to `vc-x1`.
     I wonder if we should something like `workspace-path: <self-relative-path-to-workspace-repo>`
     for vc-x1 it would be `workspace-path: ./` and for .claude it would be `workspace-path: ../`?

file.content:
## Use footers to track changeIDs or notes

Commit footers can reference changeIDs and notes files using markdown
reference-link syntax. See [ChangeID footer syntax](#changeid-footer-syntax)
for the path rules.

Since jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time (not stored in the
repo), changeID references are only resolvable by tools that have access
to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).

## ChangeID footer syntax

Commit footers use markdown reference-link syntax to cross-reference
jj changeIDs across repos:

- `/changeID` — workspace-root repo (e.g. `/upxmvpyz`)
- `/<path>/changeID` — sub-repo within the workspace (e.g. `/.claude/vxmpmzsy`)

All changeID references must be workspace-root relative (start with `/`).

## Create a binary that lists jj info

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`

content.text:
So currently we have a "ChangeID footer syntax" section using markdown reference-link style. The task is to migrate to proper git trailer format (`key: value`), which we've already started using with `ochid:`.

A few things to sort out before I start:

1. **`workspace-path:` trailer** — Your idea of each repo self-identifying. This makes `ochid` paths unambiguous since a reader can resolve relative to workspace root. Do you want this added?

2. **Scope** — Is this primarily a documentation task (update chores-01.md to describe the new git trailer convention, replacing the old markdown reference-link syntax), or also code changes (e.g. updating `vc-x1` binary to parse trailers)?

3. **Single-step or multi-step** version bump? This seems like a docs-focused change, so single-step `0.3.0` might suffice.

What's your thinking?

message.content:
1) Yes, but it seems redundant on every commit, I think we need a local .vc-config in each repo?
2) ATM no code changes as this will likely evolve so just docs
3) Single-step

content.text:
Good call — a `.vc-config` per repo avoids redundancy. Here's the plan:

**Single-step to 0.3.0:**
1. Bump version in Cargo.toml
2. Create `.vc-config` in both repos (vc-x1 and .claude) with `workspace-path`
3. Update docs in chores-01.md: document git trailer convention (`ochid:`), `.vc-config`, and deprecate the old markdown reference-link syntax
4. Update todo (move item to done)
5. Pre-commit checklist, then present commits for approval

Approve this plan?

navigate and synchronize the two repos.

## Use footers to track changeIDs or notes

Commit footers can reference changeIDs and notes files using markdown
reference-link syntax. See [ChangeID footer syntax](#changeid-footer-syntax)
for the path rules.

Since jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time (not stored in the
repo), changeID references are only resolvable by tools that have access
to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).

## ChangeID footer syntax

Commit footers use markdown reference-link syntax to cross-reference
jj changeIDs across repos:

- `/changeID` — workspace-root repo (e.g. `/upxmvpyz`)

input.new_string:
## Use footers to track changeIDs or notes

> **Note:** The original approach used markdown reference-link syntax
> in commit footers. We now use
> [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
> (`key: value` format) instead, as they are a standard convention
> and parseable by `git interpret-trailers` and other tools.

Since jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time (not stored in the
repo), changeID references are only resolvable by tools that have access
to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).

## Git trailer convention

We use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
in commit messages for inter-repo cross-references. Trailers appear as
blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of the commit body.

### ochid (Other Change ID)

The `ochid` trailer links a commit to its counterpart in another repo
within the workspace. The value is a workspace-root-relative path
followed by the jj changeID:

- `ochid: /changeID` — references the workspace-root repo (vc-x1)
- `ochid: /.claude/changeID` — references the .claude sub-repo

Example commit message:
```
Add jj tips for git users to README

Add jj tips section to README explaining why jj log
shows fewer commits than gitk.

ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo
```

### ChangeID path syntax

All changeID paths are **workspace-root relative** (start with `/`):

- `/` is the workspace root (the app repo, vc-x1)
- `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo

This means `ochid: /wtpmottv` refers to a change in the app repo,
**not** the .claude repo. The leading `/` anchors to the workspace
root, not the current repo.

### .vc-config.toml

Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
within the workspace. This avoids repeating the workspace-path in
every commit trailer.

```toml
# In vc-x1 (workspace root):
[workspace]
path = "/"

# In .claude (sub-repo):
[workspace]
path = "/.claude"
```

Tools can read `.vc-config.toml` to resolve changeID paths in
`ochid` trailers to the correct local repo.

# Chores-01

## Initial commit with dual jj-git repos

## Use commit titles to cross-reference changeIDs

This was tried but I decided footers were more flexible and less noice than the commit title.
See [ChangeID footer syntax](#changeid-footer-syntax) for the final approach.

A **critical item** in this experiment is each commit can contain the changeID
of the other commit. Here I'm trying out providing both "this commit id" (tcid)
and "other commit id" (ocid) in the commit title to make it easier for tools to
navigate and synchronize the two repos.

title syntax: `<tcid>-<ocid>-<short description>`

 - `<tcid>` is the jj change ID of this commit and is never `none`.
 - `<ocid>` is the jj change ID of the other commit and may be `none` if the bot was not involved.
 - `<short description>` is a brief one line description of the chore.

The intent is that these might be able to make the tool that shows the
evolution of the code more clear by showing the relationship between the two repos

## Use footers to track changeIDs or notes

Commit footers can reference changeIDs and notes files using markdown
reference-link syntax. See [ChangeID footer syntax](#changeid-footer-syntax)
for the path rules.

Since jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time (not stored in the
repo), changeID references are only resolvable by tools that have access
to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).

## ChangeID footer syntax

Commit footers use markdown reference-link syntax to cross-reference
jj changeIDs across repos:

- `/changeID` — workspace-root repo (e.g. `/upxmvpyz`)
- `/<path>/changeID` — sub-repo within the workspace (e.g. `/.claude/vxmpmzsy`)

All changeID references must be workspace-root relative (start with `/`).

## Create a binary that lists jj info

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`

### Implementation

Created a Rust binary (`src/main.rs`) using `jj-lib` 0.39.0 that:

1. Opens the jj workspace from the current directory (or a path argument)
2. Loads the repo at head via `Workspace::load()` and `RepoLoader::load_at_head()`
3. Evaluates `RevsetExpression::all()` to iterate all commits
4. For each commit (excluding the root), prints: changeID (reverse hex, 12 chars),
   commitID (hex, 12 chars), and first line of description

Key dependencies: `jj-lib = "0.39.0"`, `pollster = "0.4"` (for async `.block_on()`).

Usage:
```
cargo run            # uses current directory
cargo run -- /path   # uses specified path
```

lines: ## Use footers to track changeIDs or notes
-Commit footers can reference changeIDs and notes files using markdown
-reference-link syntax. See [ChangeID footer syntax](#changeid-footer-syntax)
-for the path rules.
+> **Note:** The original approach used markdown reference-link syntax
+> in commit footers. We now use
+> [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
+> (`key: value` format) instead, as they are a standard convention
+> and parseable by `git interpret-trailers` and other tools.
Since jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time (not stored in the
repo), changeID references are only resolvable by tools that have access
to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).
-## ChangeID footer syntax
+## Git trailer convention
-Commit footers use markdown reference-link syntax to cross-reference
-jj changeIDs across repos:
+We use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
+in commit messages for inter-repo cross-references. Trailers appear as
+blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of the commit body.
-- `/changeID` — workspace-root repo (e.g. `/upxmvpyz`)
-- `/<path>/changeID` — sub-repo within the workspace (e.g. `/.claude/vxmpmzsy`)
+### ochid (Other Change ID)
-All changeID references must be workspace-root relative (start with `/`).
+The `ochid` trailer links a commit to its counterpart in another repo
+within the workspace. The value is a workspace-root-relative path
+followed by the jj changeID:
+- `ochid: /changeID` — references the workspace-root repo (vc-x1)
+- `ochid: /.claude/changeID` — references the .claude sub-repo
+Example commit message:
+Add jj tips for git users to README
+Add jj tips section to README explaining why jj log
+shows fewer commits than gitk.
+ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo
+### ChangeID path syntax
+All changeID paths are **workspace-root relative** (start with `/`):
+- `/` is the workspace root (the app repo, vc-x1)
+- `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo
+This means `ochid: /wtpmottv` refers to a change in the app repo,
+**not** the .claude repo. The leading `/` anchors to the workspace
+root, not the current repo.
+### .vc-config.toml
+Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
+within the workspace. This avoids repeating the workspace-path in
+every commit trailer.
+```toml
+# In vc-x1 (workspace root):
+[workspace]
+path = "/"
+# In .claude (sub-repo):
+[workspace]
+path = "/.claude"
+Tools can read `.vc-config.toml` to resolve changeID paths in
+`ochid` trailers to the correct local repo.
## Create a binary that lists jj info
This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title

content.content:
1→# vc-x1
     2→
     3→This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
     4→We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
     5→in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
     6→initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
     7→made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
     8→and one with the conversation with the bot.
     9→
    10→I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
    11→each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
    12→of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
    13→changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
    14→between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
    15→evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
    16→most importantly **why** the code evolved.
    17→
    18→The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
    19→(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
    20→existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
    21→own IDE for vc.
    22→
    23→See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
    24→for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
    25→doing so and I then created this README.md file.
    26→
    27→## jj Tips for Git Users
    28→
    29→If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
    30→tools like `gitk --all`.
    31→
    32→### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`
    33→
    34→jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
    35→When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
    36→jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
    37→undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
    38→shows the current version of each change.
    39→
    40→### Useful commands
    41→
    42→| Command | Description |
    43→|---------|-------------|
    44→| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |

file.content:
# vc-x1

This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
and one with the conversation with the bot.

I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
most importantly **why** the code evolved.

The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
own IDE for vc.

See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
doing so and I then created this README.md file.

## jj Tips for Git Users

If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
tools like `gitk --all`.

### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`

jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
shows the current version of each change.

### Useful commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
| `jj obslog -r <change-id>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |

In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

input.new_string:
## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
and jj changeID:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
```

Paths always start with `/` (the workspace root, i.e. vc-x1).
Each repo has a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
within the workspace, so tools can resolve these paths locally.

For full details see:
- [Git trailer convention](./notes/chores-01.md#git-trailer-convention)
- [ChangeID path syntax](./notes/chores-01.md#changeid-path-syntax)
- [.vc-config.toml](./notes/chores-01.md#vc-configtoml)

## License

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

A markdown list of Tasks currently in progress


## Todo

A markdown list of task to do in the near feature

 - Use the git trailers, https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers, format for adding inter and intra repo information in commits
   - Add appropriate documentation for instance we must document throughly
     the <changeID-path> in `ochid: <changeID-path>.
     In particular `/` means workspace relative so `ochid: /wtpmottv` is not
     referencing .claude AI session repo but is refering to `vc-x1`.
     I wonder if we should something like `workspace-path: <self-relative-path-to-workspace-repo>`
     for vc-x1 it would be `workspace-path: ./` and for .claude it would be `workspace-path: ../`?
   - **Resolution**: Using `.vc-config.toml` per repo with `[workspace] path`
     instead of repeating workspace-path in every commit.
 - Determine the organization of the commits in jj and how we can iterate over them.
   - I'd like to beable to see "all" commits organized appropriately, `gitk --all` sees many more commits than any jj command I've found

## Done

See older [done.md](done.md).

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed.

- Document git trailer convention (ochid:) and .vc-config.toml for workspace identity
- Document why jj log shows fewer commits than gitk (refs/jj/keep, obslog, ::@ revset)
- Create a binary that lists jj info[[1]]


# References

A set of markdown references for tasks and details including vc changeID URLs.
See [ChangeID footer syntax](chores-01.md#changeid-footer-syntax).

[1]: /notes/chores-01.md#create-a-binary-that-lists-jj-info

   - Add appropriate documentation for instance we must document throughly

to 0.3.0. Update todo.

  -R .

jj commit \
  -m "Document git trailer convention and .vc-config.toml" \
  -m "Session: documented git trailer convention (ochid:),
created .vc-config.toml for both repos, updated memory.

ochid: /wmxvupmt" \
  -R .claude
```
